title: Chemehuevi

infobox.value: (English: [/ˌtʃɛməˈweɪvi/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*CHEH-mə-WAY-vee*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")) are an [Indigenous people of the Great Basin](/wiki/Indigenous_people_of_the_Great_Basin "Indigenous people of the Great Basin"). They are the southernmost branch of [Southern Paiute](/wiki/Southern_Paiute "Southern Paiute").[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-sdsu-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) Today, Chemehuevi people are enrolled in the following [federally recognized tribes](/wiki/Federally_recognized_tribes "Federally recognized tribes"):
(**Hokwaits**, lived in the [Ivanpah Mountains](/wiki/Ivanpah_Mountains "Ivanpah Mountains"), called Ivanpah Mountain Group)
(lived in the area of [Ash Meadows](/wiki/Ash_Meadows "Ash Meadows"), called Ash Meadows Group)
(lived in the [Kingston Mountains](/wiki/Kingston_Mountains "Kingston Mountains"), called Kingston Mountain Group)
(**Movweats**, lived on Cottonwood Island, called Cottonwood Island Group)
((in Spanish) "the bald-headed", traveled to the area north of [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles"))
(one group of them lived at [Twentynine Palms](/wiki/Twentynine_Palms "Twentynine Palms"), the second one in Chemehuevi Valley)
(**Timpashauwagotsits**, lived in the Providence Mountains, therefore called Providence Mountain Group)
(lived in the [Amargosa Valley](/wiki/Amargosa_Valley "Amargosa Valley") and along the [Amargosa River](/wiki/Amargosa_River "Amargosa River"), called Amargosa River Group)

["2010 Census CPH-T-6. American Indian and Alaska Native Tribes in the United States and Puerto Rico: 2010"](https://web.archive.org/web/20141209093630/http://www.census.gov/population/www/cen2010/cph-t/t-6tables/TABLE%20%281%29.pdf) (PDF). Archived from [the original](https://www.census.gov/population/www/cen2010/cph-t/t-6tables/TABLE%20(1).pdf) (PDF) on 9 December 2014.

[" Northern Paiute - Religion and Expressive Culture ".](http://www.everyculture.com/North-America/Northern-Paiute-Religion-and-Expressive-Culture.html) *Countries and Their Cultures.* Retrieved 8 December 2009.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Indigenous people of the Great Basin

Ethnic group

Chemehuevi

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| Chemehuevi lands in California and Arizona | |
| Total population | |
| 2010: 1,201 alone and in combination[[1]](#cite_note-2010_census-1) | |
| Regions with significant populations | |
| [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") ( [Arizona](/wiki/Arizona "Arizona"),  [California](/wiki/California "California")) | |
| Languages | |
| English, [Colorado River Numic](/wiki/Colorado_River_Numic_language "Colorado River Numic language") (ISO 639-3, ute) | |
| Religion | |
| [Native American Church](/wiki/Native_American_Church "Native American Church"), [Sun Dance](/wiki/Sun_Dance "Sun Dance"), traditional tribal religion,[[2]](#cite_note-2) [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity"), [Ghost Dance](/wiki/Ghost_Dance "Ghost Dance") | |
| Related ethnic groups | |
| [Southern Paiute](/wiki/Southern_Paiute "Southern Paiute") people | |

The **Chemehuevi** (English: [/ˌtʃɛməˈweɪvi/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*CHEH-mə-WAY-vee*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")) are an [Indigenous people of the Great Basin](/wiki/Indigenous_people_of_the_Great_Basin "Indigenous people of the Great Basin"). They are the southernmost branch of [Southern Paiute](/wiki/Southern_Paiute "Southern Paiute").[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-sdsu-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) Today, Chemehuevi people are enrolled in the following [federally recognized tribes](/wiki/Federally_recognized_tribes "Federally recognized tribes"):

* [Colorado River Indian Tribes](/wiki/Colorado_River_Indian_Tribes "Colorado River Indian Tribes")
* [Chemehuevi Indian Tribe of the Chemehuevi Reservation](/wiki/Chemehuevi_Indian_Tribe_of_the_Chemehuevi_Reservation "Chemehuevi Indian Tribe of the Chemehuevi Reservation")
* [Morongo Band of Mission Indians](/wiki/Morongo_Band_of_Mission_Indians "Morongo Band of Mission Indians")
* [Cabazon Band of Mission Indians](/wiki/Cabazon_Band_of_Mission_Indians "Cabazon Band of Mission Indians")
* [Agua Caliente Band of Cahuilla Indians](/wiki/Agua_Caliente_Band_of_Cahuilla_Indians "Agua Caliente Band of Cahuilla Indians")[[6]](#cite_note-6)
* [Torres-Martinez Desert Cahuilla Indians](/wiki/Torres-Martinez_Desert_Cahuilla_Indians "Torres-Martinez Desert Cahuilla Indians")
* [Twenty-Nine Palms Band of Mission Indians of California](/wiki/Twenty-Nine_Palms_Band_of_Mission_Indians_of_California "Twenty-Nine Palms Band of Mission Indians of California")[[7]](#cite_note-7)

Some Chemehuevi are also part of the [Soboba Band of Luiseño Indians](/wiki/Soboba_Band_of_Luise%C3%B1o_Indians "Soboba Band of Luiseño Indians"), whose members are mostly *Sovovatum* or *Soboba band* members of [Cahuilla](/wiki/Cahuilla_people "Cahuilla people") and [Luiseño people](/wiki/Luise%C3%B1o_people "Luiseño people").

## Name

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chemehuevi&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Name")]

"Chemehuevi" has multiple interpretations. It is considered to be either a [Mojave](/wiki/Mojave_language "Mojave language") term meaning "those who play with fish"[[8]](#cite_note-8) or a [Quechan](/wiki/Quechan_language "Quechan language") word meaning "nose-in-the-air-like-a-[roadrunner](/wiki/Roadrunner "Roadrunner")."[[9]](#cite_note-p23-9) The Chemehuevi call themselves *Nüwüvi* ("The People", singular *Nüwü*)[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11) or *Tantáwats*, meaning "Southern Men."[[9]](#cite_note-p23-9) Alternate spellings of Chemehuevi include Chemeguab and Chemegueb.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

## Language

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chemehuevi&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Language")]

Their language, Chemehuevi, is a [Colorado River Numic language](/wiki/Colorado_River_Numic_language "Colorado River Numic language"), in the [Numic language](/wiki/Numic_language "Numic language") branch of the [Uto-Aztecan language](/wiki/Uto-Aztecan_language "Uto-Aztecan language") family. First transcribed by [John P. Harrington](/wiki/John_P._Harrington "John P. Harrington") and [Carobeth Laird](/wiki/Carobeth_Laird "Carobeth Laird") in the early 20th century, it was studied in the 1970s by linguist Margaret L. Press.[[13]](#cite_note-13) whose field notes and extensive sound recordings remain available.[[14]](#cite_note-14) The language is now near extinction;[[15]](#cite_note-15) during the filming of [Ironbound Films](/wiki/Ironbound_Films "Ironbound Films")' 2008 American documentary film *[The Linguists](/wiki/The_Linguists "The Linguists")*, linguists [Greg Anderson](/wiki/Greg_Anderson_(linguist) "Greg Anderson (linguist)") and [K. David Harrison](/wiki/K._David_Harrison "K. David Harrison") interviewed and recorded one of the last 3 remaining speakers.

In 2015, the Siwavaats Junior College in [Havasu Lake, California](/wiki/Havasu_Lake,_California "Havasu Lake, California"), was established to teach children the language. A Chemehuevi dictionary with 2,500 words was expected to become available in 2016.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

## History and traditional culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chemehuevi&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: History and traditional culture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Chemehuevi.jpg/250px-Chemehuevi.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chemehuevi.jpg)

McKinley Fisher, a Chemehuevi man employed by the Indian Service at Colorado Agency, Arizona in 1957.

The Chemehuevi were originally a desert tribe among the [Southern Paiute](/wiki/Southern_Paiute "Southern Paiute") group. Post-contact, they lived primarily in the eastern [Mojave Desert](/wiki/Mojave_Desert "Mojave Desert") and later [Cottonwood Island](/wiki/Cottonwood_Island_(Nevada) "Cottonwood Island (Nevada)") in [Nevada](/wiki/Nevada "Nevada") and the Chemehuevi Valley along the [Colorado River](/wiki/Colorado_River "Colorado River") in [California](/wiki/California "California"). They were a nomadic people living in small groups given the sparse resources available in the desert environment. Carobeth Laird indicates their traditional territory spanned the [High Desert](/wiki/High_Desert_(California) "High Desert (California)") from the [Colorado River](/wiki/Colorado_River "Colorado River") on the east to the [Tehachapi Mountains](/wiki/Tehachapi_Mountains "Tehachapi Mountains") on the west and from the [Las Vegas](/wiki/Metropolitan_area "Metropolitan area") area and [Death Valley](/wiki/Death_Valley "Death Valley") on the north to the [San Bernardino](/wiki/San_Bernardino_Mountains "San Bernardino Mountains") and [San Gabriel Mountains](/wiki/San_Gabriel_Mountains "San Gabriel Mountains") in the south. They are most closely identified as among the [Great Basin Indians](/wiki/Great_Basin_Indians "Great Basin Indians"). Among others they are cousins of the [Kawaiisu](/wiki/Kawaiisu "Kawaiisu").[[17]](#cite_note-17)

The most comprehensive collection of Chemehuevi history, culture and mythology was gathered by [Carobeth Laird](/wiki/Carobeth_Laird "Carobeth Laird") (1895–1983) and her second husband, George Laird, one of the last Chemehuevi to have been raised in the traditional culture. Carobeth Laird, a linguist and ethnographer, wrote a comprehensive account of the culture and language as George Laird remembered it, and published their collaborative efforts in her 1976 *The Chemehuevis*, the first – and, to date, only – ethnography of the Chemehuevi traditional culture.

Describing the Chemehuevi as she knew them, and presenting the texture of traditional life amongst the people, Carobeth Laird writes:

> The Chemehuevi character is made up of polarities which are complementary rather than contradictory. They are loquacious yet capable of silence; gregarious yet so close to the earth that single families or even men alone might live and travel for long periods away from other human beings; proud, yet capable of a gentle self-ridicule. They are conservative to a degree, yet insatiably curious and ready to inquire into and even to adopt new ways: to visit all tribes, whether friends or enemies; to speak strange tongues, sing strange songs, and marry strange wives.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

The Chemehuevi made intricately coiled baskets using a three-rod foundation of willow. Traditionally, the majority of weaving was completed with split willow, and darker patterns were made with devil's claw and yucca, among other materials.[[19]](#cite_note-19) This traditional style of basketmaking is currently practiced by a small group of weavers.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

## Population

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chemehuevi&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Population")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Chemehuevi_boy.png/250px-Chemehuevi_boy.png)](/wiki/File:Chemehuevi_boy.png)

Chemehuevi boy by [Edward S. Curtis](/wiki/Edward_S._Curtis "Edward S. Curtis")

Estimates for the [pre-contact populations](/wiki/Population_of_Native_California "Population of Native California") of most [Indigenous peoples of California](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_California "Indigenous peoples of California") have varied substantially. [Alfred L. Kroeber](/wiki/Alfred_L._Kroeber "Alfred L. Kroeber") estimated the combined 1770 population of the Chemehuevi, [Koso](/wiki/Timbisha "Timbisha"), and [Kawaiisu](/wiki/Kawaiisu "Kawaiisu") as 1,500. The combined estimate in 1910 dropped to 500.[[21]](#cite_note-21) An Indian agent reported the Chemehuevi population in 1875 to be 350.[[22]](#cite_note-22) Kroeber estimated U.S. census data put the Chemehuevi population in 1910 as 355.[[23]](#cite_note-23) Population as of 2016 is in the thousands.

## Bands

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chemehuevi&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Bands")]

* **Howaits** (**Hokwaits**, lived in the [Ivanpah Mountains](/wiki/Ivanpah_Mountains "Ivanpah Mountains"), called Ivanpah Mountain Group)
* **Kauyaichits** (lived in the area of [Ash Meadows](/wiki/Ash_Meadows "Ash Meadows"), called Ash Meadows Group)
* **Mokwats** (lived in the [Kingston Mountains](/wiki/Kingston_Mountains "Kingston Mountains"), called Kingston Mountain Group)
* **Moviats** (**Movweats**, lived on Cottonwood Island, called Cottonwood Island Group)
* **Palonies** ((in Spanish) "the bald-headed", traveled to the area north of [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles"))
* **Shivawach** (one group of them lived at [Twentynine Palms](/wiki/Twentynine_Palms "Twentynine Palms"), the second one in Chemehuevi Valley)
* **Tümplsagavatsits** (**Timpashauwagotsits**, lived in the Providence Mountains, therefore called Providence Mountain Group)
* **Yagats** (lived in the [Amargosa Valley](/wiki/Amargosa_Valley "Amargosa Valley") and along the [Amargosa River](/wiki/Amargosa_River "Amargosa River"), called Amargosa River Group)

## See also

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chemehuevi&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: See also")]

* [Chemehuevi traditional narratives](/wiki/Chemehuevi_traditional_narratives "Chemehuevi traditional narratives")
* [Classification of Indigenous peoples of the Americas](/wiki/Classification_of_Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Classification of Indigenous peoples of the Americas")
